Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd try to set aside time on weekends since I work part-time during the week, but it never really stuck to a consistent pattern. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time making diagrams and color-coded notes from the lecture materials, but the actual when and where of studying shifted constantly. With seven online courses, I was always juggling something.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines. I'd have these bursts of activity when assignments were due, then kind of coast in between. Sometimes I wouldn't touch course materials for days, then suddenly I'd be up late cramming everything in. It wasn't ideal, but between my extracurriculars and limited resources, I had to work with what I had.
Q3: Usually it was an assignment deadline or a notification that something was due soon. I wasn't the type to log in just to browse around or check things casually. Sometimes I'd remember during a mindfulness session that I needed to check something, and that would prompt me to log in. But mostly it was reactive rather than proactive.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became something I naturally gravitated toward. I'd use it when I had to, but it always felt like a chore rather than a habit. There were a few weeks where I tried to make it part of my morning routine, but that fell apart pretty quickly when life got busy. It just never clicked for me the way other tools did.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the visual content at my own pace. I watched a lot of those videos, actually. The discussion posts were occasionally helpful when other students explained concepts in different ways. But honestly, I found myself converting everything into my own visual notes rather than relying on the LMS format directly.
Q6: I mostly ignored the extra reading materials and some of the supplementary resources. With limited internet at home sometimes and not much money for printing, I had to be selective about what I engaged with. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, it usually got pushed to the bottom of my priority list and stayed there.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my grades or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. Visual resources, clear explanations, and anything that would help me on exams—those felt valuable. Busy work or things that seemed designed just to keep us occupied? Those felt like a waste when I had so much else going on.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. Required ones got done because they had to, but optional stuff? I'd look at it and think about all the other things competing for my time and energy. Sometimes I felt guilty about skipping them, but with seven courses and everything else in my life, I had to make choices.
Q9: Oh, absolutely. I downloaded lecture slides and videos when I could, then studied them offline constantly. I'd make handwritten visual notes, draw concept maps, and review everything away from my computer. A lot of my actual learning happened with paper and colored pens spread out on my bed, not logged into any system.
Q10: Most of my real studying happened offline after I'd grabbed the materials. I'd spend hours working through concepts with my notes, testing myself, making visual summaries—none of which would show up anywhere. The LMS would just show that I downloaded something once, but that one download might represent ten hours of actual study time.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a video or document once, download it or take screenshots, and then that was it for my LMS activity on that item. But I'd return to those materials over and over again offline. My exam scores reflect that I actually learned the content, even if my login frequency doesn't show it.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when everything seemed to pile up at once. My stress levels were pretty high, and even though I'm generally resilient, there were weeks where I felt stretched too thin. The mindfulness practice helped me cope, but it didn't magically create more hours in the day.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed with multiple deadlines across seven courses, the LMS became just another thing on my to-do list that I'd rush through. I'd grab what I needed as quickly as possible and log out. Fatigue especially made it hard to engage meaningfully—I'd be reading but not really absorbing anything.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be scrolling through materials or watching videos but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be thinking about money stress or what I needed to do for my other courses. Physically I was engaging with the LMS, but mentally I was checked out. Those sessions didn't really count as learning.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot because they felt less urgent than assignments with hard deadlines. I knew participating was important for understanding, but when I had to choose between writing a discussion post and finishing an assignment worth more of my grade, the choice was obvious. I'd tell myself I'd catch up later, but later rarely came.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage pattern. I'd spike in activity right before things were due, then drop off completely afterward. It wasn't healthy, but it was how I survived managing so many courses. The deadline was the trigger that finally pushed me to engage, for better or worse.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, but often with a delay. If I got good feedback, I'd feel motivated for a bit, but that energy would fade. Critical feedback sometimes made me anxious and I'd avoid looking at similar assignments for a while. Eventually I'd process it and try to improve, but my immediate reaction wasn't always productive.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty standard—modules, deadlines, the usual. It didn't particularly encourage me to engage more or less. I think I would have benefited from more visual elements and maybe smaller, more frequent check-ins rather than big assignments. But I adapted to what was there and made it work.
Q19: Having clear deadlines and organized modules made it easier to know what needed to be done. But the sheer volume of content across all my courses made it harder to engage deeply with any single one. More visual summaries or infographics would have helped someone like me who learns better that way.
Q20: I think those students might be searching for something, trying to understand what's expected, or feeling lost about where to focus. They could also be dealing with anxiety about the work—looking at everything but feeling paralyzed about actually starting. Or maybe they're just procrastinating by browsing instead of doing.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. I didn't log in frequently, but I studied a lot offline and performed reasonably well. The LMS activity doesn't capture the hours I spent with printed notes and hand-drawn diagrams. Low activity doesn't mean low effort—it might just mean different study methods.
Q22: A course log can show when you accessed things and how often, which gives some picture of engagement patterns. But it completely misses offline studying, the quality of attention during online sessions, and whether someone actually understood what they were looking at. It's like measuring only the tip of the iceberg.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm genuinely curious and making connections between concepts, when I'm thinking about the content even when I'm not actively studying. It's mental investment, not just time spent logged into a system.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on visual learning strategies offline, or how the mindfulness intervention actually helped me manage my stress and stay functional. It wouldn't capture the financial stress affecting my choices, or how I balanced seven courses with limited resources. The numbers tell such a small part of the story.
